@anthropic-ai/sdk.omission_reason: messages.batches.retrieve() is an idempotent polling GET with no distinct error contract beyond the standard APIError hierarchy already covered by batches.create(). messages.batches.results() is a streaming JSONL download — same error profile as retrieve, plus network streaming errors identical to messages.stream() already contracted. beta.files.delete/download/list/retrieveMetadata are admin or read-only operations rarely called in SaaS app runtime code. models.retrieve/models.list are read-only GETs with no meaningful domain-specific error contracts. completions.create() is the deprecated legacy Text Completions API — Anthropic explicitly recommends migrating to Messages API and future models will not support it.
@anthropic-ai/sdk.notes: 4 new functions contracted: countTokens (2 postconditions: no-try-catch + estimate-as-exact), batches.create (3 postconditions: no-try-catch + result-not-polled + individual-errors-not-handled), beta.files.upload (2 postconditions: no-try-catch + id-not-validated), messages.parse (2 postconditions: no-try-catch + null-output-unchecked). 4 scanner concerns queued (concern-20260402-anthropic-sdk-deepen-1 through -4). Key new insights: (1) countTokens has independent rate limits separate from messages.create; (2) batches produce individual result statuses (succeeded/errored/canceled/expired) that must be checked per-item; (3) beta.files.upload has storage quota errors (403) distinct from standard HTTP errors; (4) messages.parse returns parsed_output as T | null — callers must null-check before use.

functions_intentionally_omitted: GetBucket* (ACL, CORS, Encryption, IntelligentTiering, Inventory, LifecycleConfiguration, Location, Logging, MetricsConfiguration, NotificationConfiguration, OwnershipControls, Policy, PolicyStatus, Replication, RequestPayment, Tagging, Versioning, Website)

@aws-sdk/client-s3.omission_reason: Admin/config GET/PUT/DELETE commands for bucket configuration (CORS, encryption, lifecycle, ACL, etc.) are infrastructure management operations rarely called in app code. Object tagging, versioning metadata, and legal hold operations are compliance admin ops. SelectObjectContentCommand (S3 Select) and WriteGetObjectResponseCommand (Object Lambda) are niche APIs. CreateSessionCommand is S3 Express One Zone — very new, limited adoption. All these have no unique behavioral contracts beyond generic HTTP error handling. ListParts/ListMultipartUploads/ListObjectVersions are pagination utilities covered by list-operation postcondition.
previously_contracted: send (object operations: GetObject, PutObject, DeleteObject, HeadObject, CopyObject)
@aws-sdk/client-s3.notes: 5 new function entries added this pass. Key insights: (1) DeleteObjectsCommand has a critical silent failure pattern — individual object deletion failures go to response.Errors[], not thrown, so code that doesn't check response.Errors silently loses data. (2) waitUntilObjectExists/waitUntilBucketExists throw TimeoutError (confirmed from @smithy/util-waiter source) — upload confirmation flows crash without try-catch. (3) AbortMultipartUploadCommand used in cleanup paths also throws NoSuchUpload — must be wrapped in its own try-catch. (4) RestoreObjectCommand throws ObjectAlreadyInActiveTierError (409) and GlacierExpeditedRetrievalNotAvailable (503). Scanner concern queued for response-pattern detection on DeleteObjectsCommand batch deletes.

@aws-sdk/client-ses.omission_reason: VerifyEmailIdentityCommand and VerifyDomainIdentityCommand are one-time setup operations with no distinct error contracts beyond generic SESServiceException. DeleteTemplateCommand, GetTemplateCommand, ListTemplatesCommand, GetSendQuotaCommand, GetSendStatisticsCommand are read-only or idempotent admin operations with no behavioral contracts beyond SESServiceException already covered by the generic send() postcondition.
@aws-sdk/client-ses.notes: 7 new functions contracted this pass. Key insights: (1) CreateReceiptRuleCommand validates Lambda/S3/SNS action targets at rule-creation time — InvalidLambdaFunctionException, InvalidS3ConfigurationException, InvalidSnsTopicException thrown before any email is received, catching misconfigured IAM permissions early; (2) SetActiveReceiptRuleSetCommand silently fails to switch inbound routing when RuleSetDoesNotExistException is swallowed — previous rule set stays active but intended routing change is lost; (3) UpdateConfigurationSetSendingEnabledCommand is the primary automated reputation protection mechanism — if the Lambda that calls this crashes due to ConfigurationSetDoesNotExistException, bounces continue accumulating and may trigger account-level suspension; (4) PutIdentityPolicyCommand InvalidPolicyException includes detailed error message from AWS about policy syntax issues; (5) DeleteConfigurationSetEventDestinationCommand is NOT idempotent unlike DeleteObject in S3 — cleanup scripts must catch EventDestinationDoesNotExistException.

@clerk/nextjs.omission_reason: clerkClient() is a factory method — contracted via its sub-methods. verifyToken() is a low-level JWT utility rarely called directly. getAuth() is the Pages Router equivalent of auth() with identical error profile. useAuth/useSession/useOrganization/useOrganizationList/useSessionList/useEmailLink/useWaitlist/useReverification/useOrganizationCreationDefaults are React hooks that return state objects — they do not perform async operations or throw. signIn.attemptSecondFactor has identical error profile to attemptFirstFactor. signUp.prepareVerification/attemptVerification share the error profile with signUp.create. clerkClient().users.updateUser/getUser have identical ClerkAPIResponseError profile to createUser. buildClerkProps/createRouteMatcher/createFrontendApiProxyHandlers/createClerkClient are sync factories or utilities. reverificationErrorResponse is a utility to create error responses.
@clerk/nextjs.notes: Pass 2: Added clerkFrontendApiProxy() with 3 postconditions covering env var misconfiguration (proxy-missing-publishable-key, proxy-missing-secret-key) and network failure (proxy-network-failure-returns-502). Key insight: clerkFrontendApiProxy returns error Response objects (500/502) instead of throwing — requires new detector patterns (env-var-presence, response-status-check) rather than try-catch detection. Also expanded omissions to include 10 additional React hooks and sync utilities that were not explicitly categorized in pass 1. 2 scanner concerns queued.

@elastic/elasticsearch.omission_reason: knnSearch is deprecated — replaced by knn option in search(). sql.query is the legacy SQL interface, rarely used in SaaS apps that have migrated to esql. helpers.msearch is an advanced batching helper with identical error profile to msearch() — covered by msearch contract. helpers.esql is a transformation wrapper around esql.query() — covered by esql.query contract. helpers.scrollDocuments has identical error profile to helpers.scrollSearch (both return AsyncIterable) — one contract covers both. ping() is a health-check utility with no business data impact.
@elastic/elasticsearch.notes: 6 new function entries added this pass. Key insights: (1) msearch() resolves HTTP 200 with ErrorResponseBase objects in responses[] for per-search failures — confirmed from MsearchResponseItem type in types.d.ts; (2) closePointInTime() and clearScroll() are resource-cleanup methods requiring finally-block placement; (3) reindex() has same partial-operation failure pattern as deleteByQuery/updateByQuery (response.failures[]); (4) esql.query() syntax errors manifest as runtime ResponseError(400); (5) helpers.scrollSearch() uses async iterator — for-await-of loop needs enclosing try-catch. 6 scanner concerns queued.

@prisma/client.omission_reason: findMany/count/aggregate/groupBy are read-only operations that return empty arrays/0 (not null/throw), with no meaningful domain-specific error contracts beyond generic connection errors. $queryRawUnsafe/$executeRawUnsafe are explicitly unsafe variants that share the same error space as $queryRaw/$executeRaw but the developer has already opted in to unsafe mode. createManyAndReturn/updateManyAndReturn are convenience variants of createMany/updateMany with identical error profiles.

@slack/web-api.omission_reason: conversations.info/members/replies and chat.getPermalink are read-only GETs with no error contracts beyond generic HTTP. users.profile.get is a read-only GET. views.push/publish/update have identical error profiles to views.open which is contracted. oauth.v2.access is an OAuth flow method rarely called in user app code (handled by framework/middleware).

@supabase/supabase-js.omission_reason: channel/removeChannel/removeAllChannels are Realtime subscription event-based APIs with no meaningful throw/return error contracts (they use callbacks). schema() is a builder method with no own errors. signInAnonymously is rarely used in SaaS apps (typically disabled). resend() and updateUser() have identical error profiles to signUp/signInWithPassword already contracted.

client.createMultipartUpload (same error profile as server createMultipartUpload)

@vercel/blob.omission_reason: Client-side versions of server multipart functions (client.createMultipartUpload, client.uploadPart, client.completeMultipartUpload, client.createMultipartUploader) share identical error profiles with their server counterparts already contracted. client.put() with explicit token has the same error profile as upload() (which also uses a client token). getDownloadUrl() and getPayloadFromClientToken() are synchronous utilities with no async error surface.
@vercel/blob.notes: Pass 10 (deepen-stream-2 2026-04-16): 4 new functions contracted. Full API surface enumerated from dist/index.d.ts and dist/client.d.ts: 16 async-callable functions total. Key insights: (1) upload() (client) throws BlobError if handleUploadUrl is missing (sync, before any network call), BlobError if server route returns non-2xx or non-JSON, and BlobClientTokenExpiredError if token TTL exceeded — common in SPAs where user spends >1hr before uploading; (2) generateClientTokenFromReadWriteToken() MUST be server-side only — throws BlobError if window is defined (browser detection), critical security: exposes BLOB_READ_WRITE_TOKEN if called client-side; (3) createMultipartUploader() is a convenience wrapper that manages key+uploadId state internally — same initial error profile as createMultipartUpload() but returned uploader.uploadPart() is NOT auto-wrapped in try-catch by users; (4) createFolder() creates a trailing-slash zero-byte blob — Vercel Blob has no real folders, this is purely for file browser UX. 4 scanner concerns queued for upload(), generateClientTokenFromReadWriteToken(), createMultipartUploader(), createFolder().

ai.omission_reason: experimental_generateVideo is an experimental API with limited real-world adoption and no provider implementations in the corpus repos. NoVideoGeneratedError follows the identical pattern as NoImageGeneratedError — same error class hierarchy, same retry logic.

functions_intentionally_omitted: searchForHits (thin type-narrowing wrapper over search(), identical error profile)
searchForFacets (thin type-narrowing wrapper over search(), identical error profile)
algoliasearch.omission_reason: searchForHits/searchForFacets are thin wrappers that call this.search() directly — no distinct error contract. partialUpdateObjectsWithTransformation and replaceAllObjectsWithTransformation have the same configuration error profile as saveObjectsWithTransformation (which is contracted) — requiring the same transformation.region check. Contracting all three separately would duplicate the same postcondition without adding insight.
algoliasearch.notes: Pass 9 (deepen-stream-2): Added 5 functions this pass. Key insights: (1) partialUpdateObjects (plural helper) is distinct from partialUpdateObject (singular) — uses chunkedBatch internally; createIfNotExists=false silently skips missing objects with no indication in response; (2) addOrUpdateObject throws synchronous native Error for missing required params BEFORE network request — must handle both sync and async errors; (3) indexExists internally catches 404 and returns false, but 401/403/RetryError still propagate — false safety assumption causes uncaught auth errors; (4) waitForAppTask throws native Error 'The maximum number of retries exceeded. (50/50)' — NOT an ApiError; callers handling only ApiError will miss timeout; (5) saveObjectsWithTransformation throws synchronous Error when transformation.region not set at client init — configuration error. 5 scanner concerns queued (deepen-1 through -5). effective_coverage now 1.0 (20/20 non-omitted functions).

axios.omission_reason: options() is almost never called directly by application code — browsers issue OPTIONS requests automatically as CORS preflight, and Node.js server code rarely uses OPTIONS explicitly. Its error profile is identical to get/delete/head.

bcrypt.omission_reason: All 7 bcrypt API functions are contracted. No functions omitted.

bullmq.omission_reason: Worker.waitUntilReady() is a test utility. Worker.getNextJob() is an advanced manual-fetching pattern. FlowProducer.getFlow() is a read-only getter. FlowProducer.close()/QueueEvents.run()/QueueEvents.close() have identical error profiles to already-contracted Queue.close()/Worker.run(). Queue.remove()/removeJobScheduler() are cleanup ops with identical Redis error profile to add(). Job.updateData()/updateProgress()/log()/promote()/changeDelay()/changePriority() all share identical Redis error profiles with no domain-specific contracts beyond generic connection errors. Job.extendLock()/moveToFailed() are internal worker operations rarely called by application code.
bullmq.notes: 13 new function entries added this pass. Key insights: (1) Worker.run() requires error listener attached BEFORE start — missing listener causes Node.js unhandled error crash, explicitly warned in BullMQ docs; (2) FlowProducer.add() is atomic all-or-nothing via Redis MULTI/EXEC — partial flow creation is impossible but Redis failure loses entire flow silently; (3) upsertJobScheduler() failure during deployment means zero recurring jobs until next successful registration — silent cron death; (4) obliterate() throws on active jobs unless force=true — SCAN-based iteration on large queues can fail mid-obliteration; (5) Job.waitUntilFinished() rejects on both timeout AND job failure — commonly used in request-response patterns where unhandled rejection hangs HTTP requests. 4 scanner concerns queued: FlowProducer instance tracking, Worker.run() error-listener detection, Queue method await_patterns, Job instance method tracking.

functions_intentionally_omitted: Malformed urlencoded form (internal error - very rare, covered by busboy-001 error handler)
busboy.omission_reason: Malformed urlencoded form error propagated via stream write callback (cb(new Error(...))) — this is an internal stream mechanism covered generically by busboy-001 error handler. Not worth a separate postcondition as it only occurs with extremely malformed percent-encoding that no real-world client would send.
busboy.notes: 4 new postconditions added this pass. Key insights: (1) busboy-006 (field.valueTruncated) — the 1MB default fieldSize limit is commonly exceeded by JSON blobs in text fields; silent truncation is a DATA_LOSS risk; (2) busboy-007 (Unexpected end of form) — most common production busboy error, occurs on client disconnect/mobile network switch; covered by busboy-001 error handler but now explicitly documented with specific error messages; (3) busboy-008 (Multipart boundary not found) — third distinct constructor throw, occurs when Content-Type is multipart/form-data but boundary parameter is absent; covered by busboy-002 try/catch; (4) busboy-009 (field.nameTruncated) — 100-byte field name limit silently truncates dynamic field names; INFO severity as impact is limited to unusual use cases.

cassandra-driver.omission_reason: executeGraph() is the DSE Graph API, not Cassandra CQL — not present in SaaS app codebases. getReplicas()/getState() are read-only metadata helpers with no error contracts beyond connection failure already covered by connect(). Mapper.batch() is an advanced multi-model batch API rarely called in SaaS code, with identical error profile to ModelMapper operations. ModelMapper.findAll() and mapWithQuery() have identical error profiles to find() which is already contracted.
cassandra-driver.notes: 6 new function entries added this pass covering Mapper ORM layer and executeConcurrent bulk op. Key insight: executeConcurrent() has dual error behavior — raiseOnFirstError=true (default) throws on first error, raiseOnFirstError=false silently accumulates errors in result.errors[] up to maxErrors. 3 scanner concerns queued: mapper-get-no-try-catch (ModelMapper.get() not recognized), mapper-get-null-not-checked (null return type analysis needed), execute-concurrent-errors-not-checked (option-argument-aware result check analysis needed). 3 of 14 SHOULD_FIRE ground-truth tests currently failing due to missing scanner detectors (acceptable per Phase 4 rules).

cloudinary.omission_reason: remove_all_tags/remove_all_context/replace_tag share identical error profiles with already-contracted add_tag/remove_tag (same execute_request.js codepath, same HTTP codes, same plain object error shape). No additional behavioral contracts needed.
cloudinary.notes: 3 new functions contracted: update_metadata (2 postconditions: missing-error-handling + silent-partial-failure when public_ids do not exist), api.update (1 postcondition: missing-error-handling for 404/401/403/420), api.delete_resources_by_tag (2 postconditions: missing-error-handling + catastrophic-misuse for scope bleed). Key insights: (1) update_metadata silently omits non-existent public_ids from result instead of throwing 404; (2) api.update targets Admin API — needs api_key+api_secret, not just upload key; (3) delete_resources_by_tag is irreversible bulk delete — scope verification via resources_by_tag() is critical before calling.

functions_intentionally_omitted: diff (produces NaN for invalid dates but no distinct throw contract — same root cause as dayjs() invalid-date postcondition, covered by isValid() check pattern)
dayjs.omission_reason: diff() with an invalid Day.js object produces NaN which propagates silently, but the behavioral contract is identical to the existing dayjs-invalid-date postcondition — the root fix is always isValid() before use. No distinct new error type or throw behavior. Other Dayjs methods (year/month/date/hour/minute/second/ms, add/subtract, startOf/endOf, isBefore/isAfter/isSame, clone, locale, unix) are synchronous accessors/manipulators with no distinct throw contracts beyond the invalid-date pattern already contracted.
dayjs.notes: 2 new functions contracted this pass via source inspection. Key insights: (1) toISOString() is the hidden throw in Day.js — all other methods handle invalid dates gracefully (format returns "Invalid Date" string, toJSON returns null, valueOf returns NaN) but toISOString() calls native Date.prototype.toISOString() with NO isValid() guard, which throws RangeError: Invalid time value. This is a dangerous asymmetry that surprises developers. (2) dayjs.duration() with invalid ISO 8601 strings silently produces 0ms duration — confirmed from plugin source: unmatched ISO 8601 regex falls through to return this with ={} and =0, indistinguishable from dayjs.duration(0). Critical for subscription/billing code that parses duration strings from config or DB.

drizzle-orm.omission_reason: selectDistinct/selectDistinctOn have identical error profile to select() — same connection errors, same constraint errors. refreshMaterializedView() is a maintenance operation rarely called in SaaS app code. $count() returns 0 on empty result (not null/throw) with no meaningful domain-specific error contract beyond connection error already covered by select.

express.omission_reason: express.raw() and express.text() are built-in body parsing middleware with identical error profile (entity.too.large, charset.unsupported, entity.parse.failed) to express.json() — same underlying body-parser/raw-body code path. app.param() uses the same async callback pattern as app.use() middleware — errors from async param callbacks propagate identically (unhandled rejection in Express 4, auto-caught in Express 5). Already covered by async-middleware-unhandled-rejection postcondition.
express.notes: 7 new function entries added this pass. Key insights: (1) express.json() errors (SyntaxError 400, PayloadTooLarge 413) come from body-parser/raw-body with specific error.type values (entity.parse.failed, entity.too.large, charset.unsupported) — must be caught by 4-param error middleware; (2) res.sendFile/download use pillarjs/send which maps fs errors: ENOENT->404, ENAMETOOLONG->404, other->500, dotfile->403; (3) app.listen() delegates to http.Server.listen() — EADDRINUSE is the #1 Express startup failure; (4) res.render() errors depend on the template engine but Express wraps them in a standard callback(err, html) pattern. 5 scanner concerns queued: 3 uncovered functions (sendFile, download, render) + 2 false positives (express.json sync factory registration, app.listen with server.on error handler).

functions_intentionally_omitted: buildCheckFunction (identical error profile to body()/check() — creates same validation chain, no distinct behavioral contract)
Result.array() (pure data access, returns array of formatted errors — no error contracts)
Result.mapped() (pure data access, returns object keyed by field — no error contracts; first-error-per-field behavior is noteworthy but not a behavioral violation)
Result.formatWith() (returns new Result with new formatter — utility/transform, no error contracts)
validationResult.withDefaults() (factory that returns a new validationResult function — contracted in previous pass via withdefaults-formatter-throws)
express-validator.omission_reason: buildCheckFunction creates ValidationChain instances with the same error profile as body()/check() — no distinct behavioral contract. Result methods (array, mapped, isEmpty, formatWith) are pure data access utilities with no error contracts beyond the fact that validationResult() was called. validationResult.withDefaults() was contracted in the previous pass.
express-validator.notes: 2 new functions contracted this pass. Key insights: (1) matchedData() has a critical silent failure pattern when called without prior validationResult().isEmpty() check — invalid fields are silently absent (onlyValidData:true by default), so DB writes using spread of matchedData() silently use undefined for invalid fields; (2) matchedData() with optional fields requires { includeOptionals: true } or else optional fields that were not submitted are absent (silently skips clearing them in PATCH/PUT scenarios); (3) ExpressValidator custom sanitizers MUST return a value — missing return silently sets field to undefined with no validation error thrown; (4) ExpressValidator async validators treat infrastructure errors (DB timeout, network failure) as validation failures, silently consuming the infra error and returning 422 instead of 500.

firebase.omission_reason: signInWithPhoneNumber/linkWithPhoneNumber/updatePhoneNumber are SMS-only flows requiring phone number infrastructure — niche in standard SaaS apps. getDocFromServer/getDocsFromServer have identical error contracts to getDoc/getDocs (already contracted) with no distinct behavioral requirements. fetchSignInMethodsForEmail is deprecated in newer Firebase versions. validatePassword is an optional policy enforcement feature with no production error contract beyond generic auth errors.
firebase.notes: 10 new functions contracted this pass: signInWithEmailLink (1 postcondition: email-link-expired-or-invalid — expired-action-code/invalid-action-code/invalid-email codes), signInAnonymously (1: anonymous-auth-not-enabled-or-quota-exceeded — operation-not-allowed/quota-exceeded), updateProfile (1: update-profile-error — network-request-failed/user-token-expired), linkWithCredential (1: link-credential-conflict — credential-already-in-use/account-exists-with-different-credential/requires-recent-login), reauthenticateWithCredential (1: reauth-credential-error — wrong-password/user-mismatch/invalid-credential), verifyBeforeUpdateEmail (1: verify-before-update-email-error — email-already-in-use/requires-recent-login), writeBatch.commit (1: batch-commit-permission-denied — permission-denied/not-found/resource-exhausted), getCountFromServer (1: count-from-server-error — permission-denied/failed-precondition/resource-exhausted), deleteObject (1: delete-object-error — storage/object-not-found/storage/unauthorized), httpsCallable (1: functions-call-unauthenticated-or-permission-denied — functions/unauthenticated/functions/permission-denied/functions/not-found/functions/internal). Error codes confirmed from: @firebase/auth/dist/auth.d.ts AuthErrorCode enum, @firebase/functions/dist/functions-public.d.ts FunctionsErrorCode type, @firebase/storage/dist/storage-public.d.ts StorageErrorCode, @firebase/firestore/dist/index.d.ts FirestoreErrorCode. 1 scanner concern queued (httpsCallable requires 2-step callable factory detection pattern).

firebase-admin.omission_reason: getUserByPhoneNumber/getUserByEmail/getUsers share identical error profile with getUser. listUsers/deleteUsers are batch variants of single ops. sendEachForMulticast has same surface as sendEach. generateEmailVerificationLink shares surface with generatePasswordResetLink. unsubscribeFromTopic shares surface with subscribeToTopic. AppCheck.createToken/verifyToken rarely called directly in SaaS backends.
firebase-admin.notes: Depth pass 2026-04-06 (deepen-stream-2): Added 9 functions covering Auth session cookies, custom tokens, token revocation, password reset, Firestore transactions/batches, and modern messaging APIs. Ground-truth tests: 44/44 pass. Scanner correctly detects all 9 new function types via await_patterns. deepen-index.json corrected 2026-04-16: contract.yaml shows 1.0 coverage (44 contracted, 24 omitted, 68 total), index was stale at 0.74.

emptyDir (self-handles readdir errors, creates dir if missing)
ensureLink (niche filesystem op, rare in SaaS)
ensureSymlink (niche filesystem op, rare in SaaS)
fs-extra.key_findings: writeJson is a critical footgun — unlike outputFile and outputJson, it does NOT create parent directories. ENOENT when parent is missing is common. Also: remove() force:true only silences ENOENT, not EACCES/EPERM — silent failures in cleanup code.
scanner_concerns_resolved: concern-20260403-fs-extra-1 (mkdirp/mkdirs detection — scanner already detects these)
concern-20260403-fs-extra-2 (createFile detection — scanner already detects this)
fs-extra.notes: 3 new function entries added: mkdirp, mkdirs, createFile — all are aliases for existing contracted functions. mkdirp and mkdirs are aliases for ensureDir (same makeDir function reference, confirmed from lib/mkdirs/index.js). createFile is an alias for ensureFile (same function reference, confirmed from lib/ensure/file.js). Ground-truth tests: all 51 pass including 9 new SHOULD_FIRE annotations for the alias names. Scanner upgrade concerns 1 and 2 marked resolved — scanner already detects mkdirp/mkdirs/createFile. Coverage: 0.82 → 1.0 (12/12 non-omitted functions contracted).

ioredis.omission_reason: scan/hscan/sscan/zscan are cursor-based streaming operations — they are used via scanStream() helper in ioredis which wraps the low-level cursor. Contracting the low-level cursor commands would duplicate coverage. xadd/xread are Redis Streams commands with very limited SaaS adoption — rarely called in corpus repos.

jsonwebtoken.omission_reason: All 3 callable functions are contracted. No omissions.
jsonwebtoken.notes: decode() contract is unique: not error handling, but security invariant. jwt.decode() never throws (returns null), but using its return value for authentication without verify() is a complete auth bypass.

functions_intentionally_omitted: diff (NaN propagation covered by format/toISOString contracts)
toDate (Invalid Date object — same pattern as valueOf NaN)
valueOf (NaN propagation — covered by format/toISOString contracts)
moment.omission_reason: diff(), toDate(), valueOf() all follow the same NaN/invalid propagation pattern that is already captured by the format-invalid-date-string and toisostring-null-for-invalid postconditions. Adding separate entries would be redundant — the behavioral contract (check isValid() before using moment output) is already documented.
moment.notes: 5 new function entries added this pass (add, subtract, min, max, fromNow). Key insights: (1) add()/subtract() on invalid moment silently propagates invalidity — the most dangerous pattern since it is the most commonly used moment operation; callers must validate before manipulation. (2) moment.min()/moment.max() counterintuitively propagate invalidity from any single invalid argument — even if 9 of 10 dates are valid, one bad date makes the entire result invalid. (3) fromNow() returns the string "Invalid date" silently for UI rendering (not an exception). All new postconditions confirmed from https://momentjs.com/docs/ official documentation. Scanner concerns already queued from 2026-04-13 pass (concerns -1 through -6).

functions_intentionally_omitted: estimatedDocumentCount (same error profile as countDocuments — network errors + MongoServerError, already contracted)
createSearchIndex / createSearchIndexes / dropSearchIndex (Atlas-only, requires MongoDB 7.0+, niche adoption in SaaS)
Db.command (raw admin command execution, not typical SaaS runtime code)
Admin.* (all admin server management operations — monitoring, database admin, not runtime)
ClientSession.endSession (called automatically by withSession/withTransaction finally block — not called directly)
Cursor methods: toArray, forEach, next, hasNext, tryNext (cursor iteration — same network error profile as find(), already contracted via find)
Db.dropDatabase (destructive admin op, rarely in SaaS runtime code)
mongodb.omission_reason: estimatedDocumentCount shares the same error hierarchy (MongoServerError, MongoNetworkError, MongoServerSelectionError) as countDocuments which is already contracted — no distinct behavioral contract. Atlas Search index operations (createSearchIndex/dropSearchIndex) are only available on MongoDB Atlas 7.0+, not applicable to self-hosted deployments used by most SaaS developers. Db.command is a low-level primitive for raw MongoDB wire protocol commands — SaaS app code uses the typed Collection/Db methods instead. Admin.* methods are server administration operations (listDatabases, ping, removeUser, validateCollection, serverStatus) for ops/monitoring code, not application runtime. ClientSession.endSession is always called by withSession/withTransaction finally blocks — application code does not call it directly. Cursor iteration methods have the same error profile as find() (which returns the cursor) — already contracted. Db.dropDatabase is an irreversible destructive operation on par with DROP DATABASE in SQL — not in hot-path SaaS runtime code.
mongodb.notes: 6 new functions contracted this pass (deepen-stream-2 pass 13). Key insights: (1) createCollection throws MongoServerError code 48 (NamespaceExists) when collection already exists — idempotent migration code must catch code 48 specifically; also throws MongoCompatibilityError for FLE2 encrypted fields on servers < 7.0 wire protocol version 21 (confirmed from create_collection.js source); (2) distinct returns [] when no documents match (NOT an error) but throws on network/server failure — callers commonly skip try-catch for "non-critical" facet queries; (3) createIndexes throws MongoServerError code 85 (IndexOptionsConflict) or code 86 (IndexKeySpecsConflict) when index definition conflicts with existing index — neither is idempotent, unlike createIndex() with same name+options; (4) dropIndexes throws MongoServerError NamespaceNotFound (code 26) when collection does not exist, unlike dropIndex() (singular) which has IndexNotFound (code 27); (5) withSession callback errors propagate unchanged — no retry, no swallow — key behavioral difference from withTransaction(); endSession errors are squashError()d so they never mask callback errors (confirmed from mongo_client.js); (6) rename sends to admin/ internally (confirmed from rename.js ns field) — throws on TargetNamespaceExists when target collection exists and dropTarget:false; throws NamespaceNotFound (code 26) when source collection does not exist. API surface enumerated from mongodb.d.ts (v6.x) declarations.

mysql2.notes: 2 new function entries added this pass. Key insights: (1) reset() sends COM_RESET_CONNECTION and clears BOTH client-side LRU _statements cache AND server-side prepared statement scope — any PreparedStatementInfo held in application code is stale after reset(). On error, connection is unusable (must destroy(), not release()). (2) PreparedStatementInfo.close() is fire-and-forget (always resolves, no reject path) — sends COM_STMT_CLOSE which MySQL protocol does not acknowledge. The behavioral contract is resource management: not calling close() leaks server-side slots against max_prepared_stmt_count (default 16,382). Long-running services that call prepare() without close() exhibit gradual degradation hours or days later when ER_MAX_PREPARED_STMT_COUNT_REACHED hits on new prepare() calls. The note in mysql2 docs: "Manually prepared statements should be closed using statement.close()" confirms the intent.

superagent.notes: Pass 2 (2026-04-16, deepen-stream-2 pass 11): Added pipe() — 1 new function, 2 postconditions. Key insight: pipe() completely bypasses the Promise chain. Internally it calls end() and sets this.piped=true; the normal response handler checks if(this.piped)return to skip the callback/Promise path entirely. Network/HTTP errors emit as Node.js events on the Request (req.emit("error",err)); decompressor errors (gzip/deflate) emit on the destination stream (stream.emit("error",err)). try-catch and async/await provide ZERO protection. If no error listener is registered, Node.js throws an uncaught exception. Also: mixing pipe() with await (calling end() internally via then()) causes a synchronous throw: "end() has already been called, so its too late to start piping". Both postconditions confirmed from lib/node/index.js source inspection (lines 423-454, 769, 855, 889-896, 958) and https://forwardemail.github.io/superagent/#piping. 2 scanner concerns queued for detection rule implementation. Coverage: 9/9 non-omitted = 100%.

replicate.notes: Pass 8 (2026-04-16, deepen-stream-2 pass 8): First full depth pass. Enumerated complete API from replicate@1.4.0 index.d.ts and lib/ source. Added 6 new entries: stream() (no-error-handling + model-not-streaming-capable), wait() (prediction-failure as plain Error not ApiError + canceled-silent-non-throw), trainings.create() (no-error-handling + result-not-polled), files.create() (invalid-type-sync-throw + ApiError), deployments.predictions.create() (403-distinct-from-401), validateWebhook() (returns false not throws + missing-headers-throws). Key insights: (1) wait() throws plain Error not ApiError for prediction failures; (2) canceled predictions return silently without throwing; (3) validateWebhook() returns false for invalid signatures - unchecked return is security vuln; (4) stream() requires SSE support - throws after prediction is created; (5) trainings.create() returns immediately with status starting. 5 scanner concerns queued. 20/20 ground-truth tests pass.

@google/genai.notes: Pass 9 (2026-04-16, deepen-stream-2): First depth pass. Enumerated full API surface from dist/genai.d.ts (18 async-callable functions). Added 5 new function entries: sendMessage (1 postcondition: sequential chat turns with no try-catch), sendMessageStream (1 postcondition: streaming multi-turn with no try-catch), files.upload (1 postcondition: distinct non-ApiError throws for mimeType inference + VertexAI unsupported + protocol errors), countTokens (1 postcondition: pre-flight check that breaks silently if no try-catch), caches.create (1 postcondition: model-not-supported/token-threshold-not-met 400 errors). Coverage 0% → 100% of non-omitted functions (4 previously contracted + 5 new = 9 contracted, 9 omitted). Key insights: (1) chat.sendMessage() delegates to generateContent() so same ApiError surface, but uniquely stateful — failed turns recorded in comprehensive history; (2) files.upload() has 3 distinct Error (not ApiError) throw paths from the upload protocol layer; (3) caches.create() 400 error has 2 distinct causes (model-incompatible vs content-below-threshold) requiring different resolution; (4) countTokens() has independent rate limits from generateContent().

jwt-decode.omission_reason: jwt-decode exports exactly 1 callable function (jwtDecode). All exported interfaces and classes (InvalidTokenError, JwtHeader, JwtPayload, JwtDecodeOptions) are types/error classes, not callable API functions.
jwt-decode.notes: Pass 10 (2026-04-16, deepen-stream-1): First depth pass. jwt-decode has exactly 1 exported callable function (jwtDecode). It is synchronous (no async-callable functions). The existing contract already had 1 postcondition (invalid-token covering all 4 throw paths). This pass added 1 new postcondition: no-signature-validation (security misuse pattern — callers using decoded claims for access control without server-side JWT signature verification). Coverage before: 1 postcondition / 1 function = technically 100% of callable functions, but missing the security behavioral postcondition. Coverage after: 2 postconditions on 1 function = 100%. Key insight: jwt-decode README explicitly states 'This library doesn\'t validate the token' — the no-signature-validation postcondition documents the critical security contract that callers must not rely solely on jwtDecode() for auth/access decisions. Scanner concern queued for dataflow-based detection of decoded claims used in auth paths.

@auth0/nextjs-auth0.omission_reason: createFetcher() is a helper that wraps getAccessToken — its errors are already covered by Auth0Client.getAccessToken postconditions. startInteractiveLogin() returns a redirect NextResponse and has no distinct error contract beyond InvalidConfigurationError already covered by middleware. connectAccount() has low adoption and follows the same missing-session pattern as getAccessTokenForConnection which is already contracted.
@auth0/nextjs-auth0.notes: Pass 10 (2026-04-16, deepen-stream-2): First depth pass. Package was never deepened. Contract was v2/v3 only (4 standalone functions). Extended to v4 Auth0Client API (10 new functions, 23 total postconditions). Key insights: (1) getSession() returns null not throws — destructuring without null check is the #1 auth bug pattern in Next.js apps; (2) v4 getAccessToken() has a new MfaRequiredError path for MFA step-up flows that v2/v3 did not have; (3) updateSession() throws when unauthenticated (unlike getSession which returns null) — this asymmetry trips up developers; (4) MFA methods (getAuthenticators/challenge/verify/enroll) are chained via auth0.mfa.* — scanner needs multi-level property chain detection; (5) customTokenExchange() does NOT update the session — token must be stored by caller; (6) CIBA flow (getTokenByBackchannelAuth) throws two distinct error types: one before the network call (not-supported) and one after polling (denied-or-expired).

@aws-sdk/client-lambda.omission_reason: InvokeAsyncCommand is the deprecated legacy async invocation API — AWS officially deprecates it in favor of InvokeCommand with InvocationType='Event'. DeleteFunctionCommand is an admin-only destructive operation not called in SaaS app runtime code.
@aws-sdk/client-lambda.notes: 4 new functions contracted this pass: (1) UpdateFunctionCodeCommand — key insight: PreconditionFailedException (412) on RevisionId mismatch is unique to Update operations and requires distinct handling for deployment race conditions; CodeStorageExceededException (400) on 75 GB account quota. (2) UpdateFunctionConfigurationCommand — ResourceConflictException common during VPC provisioning which takes 60+ seconds; callers must waitUntilFunctionUpdated before chaining updates. (3) waitUntilPublishedVersionActive — same waiter pattern as waitUntilFunctionActive but for blue/green deploys after PublishVersionCommand; TimeoutError when version stays Pending. (4) CreateEventSourceMappingCommand — ResourceConflictException is idempotency signal (mapping already exists, safe to continue) — common pattern in SaaS deploy scripts that create SQS triggers on startup.

@azure/storage-blob.omission_reason: AppendBlobClient and PageBlobClient are niche patterns (append logs, VHD disks) with no distinct error contracts beyond generic RestError. Admin operations (setMetadata, setHTTPHeaders, setTags, setImmutabilityPolicy, setLegalHold, createSnapshot, abortCopyFromURL) are infrastructure management ops rarely in SaaS app runtime code. Safe-variant methods (*IfExists) don't throw on missing resources so need no error contract. Low-level block operations (stageBlock, commitBlockList) are covered by uploadStream's postconditions. Blob Query SQL, listBlobsFlat/ByHierarchy iterators, and read-only GET operations have no distinct behavioral contracts beyond generic HTTP error handling. BlobBatchClient operations are a niche pattern rarely used in typical SaaS app code.
@azure/storage-blob.notes: Pass 1 (2026-04-16, deepen-stream-1 pass 12): Added 10 new function entries. Key insights: (1) BlobClient.delete() SnapshotsPresent — critical silent bug where dev environments (no snapshots) succeed but production (with Azure Backup/versioning) fails with 409. (2) uploadStream() multi-block failure leaves uncommitted blocks that are auto-cleaned after 7 days but data is lost immediately. (3) beginCopyFromURL() PollerLike pattern requires explicit pollUntilDone() — not awaiting leaves destination blob in pending state. (4) downloadToFile() does NOT clean up partial files on error — callers must handle cleanup. (5) getUserDelegationKey() RBAC permission requirement (Storage Blob Delegator role) commonly missing in production. (6) generateSasUrl() throws locally (no network call) when not using StorageSharedKeyCredential — breaks teams migrating to managed identity. Coverage improved from 0% to 76% (19/48 total, 29 intentionally omitted as admin/read-only/niche ops covering only ~8% of runtime use cases).

express-session.omission_reason: touch() and resetMaxAge() are synchronous methods that reset cookie maxAge — they do not interact with the session store, make no I/O, and have no error contracts. No meaningful behavioral contract beyond their documented return value.
express-session.notes: 4 session instance methods contracted this pass. Key insights: (1) destroy() callback must check err or server-side session persists despite client-side logout (split-brain); (2) regenerate() must check err on login — failure leaves old session ID in place (session fixation); (3) save() must be called and err checked before redirects — automatic save at response end may not fire in time; (4) reload() can produce "failed to load session" when session expires in long-lived connections — must redirect to login.

@aws-sdk/lib-storage.omission_reason: on('httpUploadProgress') is synchronous event registration (returns 'this'). It is not an async function and has no error contract — the callback is called with a Progress object containing loaded/total/part fields, none of which throw. The constructor is also synchronous. These are correctly excluded from coverage calculations.
@aws-sdk/lib-storage.notes: 3 new postconditions added this pass: (1) upload-done-already-called — Upload instances are single-use; retry logic must create a new Upload instance. (2) abort-does-not-clean-up-synchronously — abort() only signals AbortController; done() must still reject before markUploadAsAborted() runs. (3) leave-parts-on-error-prevents-cleanup — leavePartsOnError=true disables automatic AbortMultipartUploadCommand, causing S3 cost accumulation. Also added: abort-before-done-is-noop (INFO) documenting that abort() before done() is safe. Key insight: Upload class is single-use by design — the 'sent' flag prevents re-execution of done().

Low-level credit card vault ops (creditCard.*) are superseded by paymentMethod.* in modern Braintree usage. braintree.notes: First deepen pass (2026-04-16, deepen-stream-1 pass 2). Added 12 new function entries. Key insights: (1) transaction.void() has a critical silent failure pattern via result.success=false — settled transactions cannot be voided but do NOT throw (they return result.success=false with error 91504). (2) transaction.submitForSettlement() is the 'capture' step in authorize-then-capture flows — failure means merchant never collects payment. (3) subscription.cancel() NotFoundError is extremely common in account-deletion flows (stale IDs, already-canceled). (4) subscription.retryCharge() has dual failure modes: thrown exception (infrastructure) AND result.success=false (card declined) — BOTH must be handled. (5) webhookNotification.parse() is security-critical: InvalidSignatureError indicates tampered payload and the handler must NOT process the notification. (6) dispute.finalize() has a 'forgot to call' anti-pattern: evidence added but finalize never called = silent loss of chargeback dispute.

square.notes: 5 new functions contracted in pass 5: catalog.batchUpsert (3 postconditions: concurrent-update 429, IDEMPOTENCY_KEY_REUSED, partial-batch-failure), disputes.accept (2 postconditions: financial-debit-immediate, deadline-missed), disputes.submitEvidence (2 postconditions: irrevocable-evidence, dashboard-exclusion), giftCards.activities.create (3 postconditions: insufficient-funds/GIFT_CARD_AVAILABLE_AMOUNT, pending-state-trap, TEMPORARY_ERROR-503), giftCards.create (2 postconditions: pending-state-trap-silent-success, invalid-GAN). google-auth-library.notes: Pass 1 (2026-04-16, deepen-stream-1 pass 4): First depth pass — 8 new functions contracted. Pass 2 (2026-04-16, deepen-stream-2 pass 6): Added JWT.fetchIdToken(). postmark.notes: 5 new functions contracted this pass. Key insights: (1) createSuppressions/deleteSuppressions return 200 OK with per-item Status field — callers must check Status even on HTTP success (silent partial failure pattern similar to AWS S3 DeleteObjectsCommand); (2) SpamComplaint suppressions cannot be deleted — deleteSuppressions always returns Status: Failed for SpamComplaint addresses; (3) createTemplate has 100-template per-server limit — throws ApiInputError when exceeded; (4) editTemplate throws ApiInputError when template alias/ID not found — common in migration scripts.

@azure/identity.omission_reason: All async-callable functions contracted. getDefaultAzureCredential() is a synchronous factory (no I/O). serializeAuthenticationRecord() and deserializeAuthenticationRecord() are synchronous helpers — deserializeAuthenticationRecord can throw on unsupported version strings but this is a narrow configuration-error case with no network I/O.
@azure/identity.notes: 2 new functions contracted this pass. Key insights: (1) getBearerTokenProvider() returns a callback that throws Error("Failed to get access token") when the underlying credential fails — the error message is generic and loses type information from CredentialUnavailableError/AuthenticationError; callers must wrap the callback in try-catch. (2) InteractiveBrowserCredential.authenticate() returns AuthenticationRecord | undefined — never throws on user cancellation (returns undefined) but DOES throw CredentialUnavailableError on OAuth infrastructure failures; callers must null-check the return value before using serializeAuthenticationRecord().

cookie-parser.omission_reason: All 5 exported functions have meaningful behavioral contracts. No omissions.
cookie-parser.notes: Depth pass 2026-04-16 (deepen-stream-2 pass 7): Added 4 new helper function entries. cookie-parser is entirely synchronous — no async functions. Key insights: (1) signedCookie() returns false (not exception) for tampered cookies — callers using truthiness check instead of === false miss security violations; (2) signedCookies() MUTATES the input cookies object by deleting signed cookie keys — destructive side-effect surprises developers who pass req.cookies directly; (3) signedCookies() puts false in returned object for invalid signatures — callers iterating without false-check silently pass tampered data; (4) JSONCookie() returns undefined (NOT the original string) for non-JSON cookies — breaks when used as parse-or-passthrough; (5) JSONCookies() mutates input in-place and returns same object reference. 5 scanner concerns queued for detection rule implementation.

multer.omission_reason: memoryStorage() is a pure factory function that returns an in-memory storage engine with no I/O operations and no distinct error surface. Memory allocation failures are OOM conditions handled at process level, not catchable per-upload.
multer.notes: 4 new function entries added. Key insights: (1) fields() throws LIMIT_UNEXPECTED_FILE when a file is received for an unlisted field or beyond maxCount — same error surface as single/array but needs separate entry for completeness; (2) any() is a critical security risk when used without fileFilter — accepts ALL files from ANY field; official docs explicitly warn against using any() as global middleware; postcondition: any-unrestricted-upload-no-filter; (3) none() throws LIMIT_UNEXPECTED_FILE when a file is included in a text-only form — missing error middleware leaves this as unhandled 500; (4) diskStorage() calls fs.mkdirSync synchronously when destination is a string — EACCES or ENOENT throws at startup; function-form destination is safer. Also: write stream errors (ENOSPC, EACCES) from DiskStorage._handleFile propagate to next(err) and require Express error middleware.

jose.omission_reason: flattenedVerify/generalVerify/flattenedDecrypt/generalDecrypt are niche JWS/JWE serialization formats with identical error contracts to the compact variants already covered. CompactEncrypt/FlattenedEncrypt/GeneralEncrypt/CompactSign/FlattenedSign/GeneralSign are lower-level builders rarely used directly — SignJWT and EncryptJWT (contracted) are the idiomatic patterns. exportSPKI/exportPKCS8/exportJWK only fail when key is non-extractable (set during generation) — a programming error caught immediately in dev, not a production runtime concern. generateSecret has no documented error cases beyond invalid alg (same contract as generateKeyPair). calculateJwkThumbprint/calculateJwkThumbprintUri are pure computation utilities with no meaningful error contracts beyond invalid key type.
jose.notes: 8 new function entries contracted. Key insights: (1) jwtDecrypt() is the encrypted-JWT equivalent of jwtVerify — throws JWEDecryptionFailed (wrong key/tampered auth tag) + JWTExpired + JWTClaimValidationFailed — commonly used in secure cookie-based session auth; (2) importSPKI/importPKCS8 are high-impact startup-time failures — PEM strings from env vars frequently have truncation/escaping issues that throw TypeError at server boot; (3) compactDecrypt() throws JWEDecryptionFailed on authentication tag failure — critical for any app using JWE for data encryption at rest; (4) compactVerify() is the lower-level JWS verification without JWT claim checks — throws JWSSignatureVerificationFailed on tampered tokens; (5) generateKeyPair() throws JOSENotSupported for unsupported algorithms or sub-2048 RSA keys — affects key rotation flows; (6) EncryptJWT.encrypt() can throw JOSENotSupported/JOSEAlgNotAllowed in sessions that use encrypted tokens. Source confirmed from panva/jose GitHub source: src/jwt/decrypt.ts, src/jwe/compact/decrypt.ts, src/jwe/flattened/decrypt.ts, src/key/import.ts, src/key/generate_key_pair.ts, src/util/errors.ts.

Its error profile (Handlebars.Exception on syntax errors) is identical to compile(), which is already contracted. parseWithoutProcessing(), registerHelper, registerPartial, create(), SafeString, and escapeExpression are sync utilities with no meaningful error contracts.
handlebars.notes: First depth pass (2026-04-16, deepen-stream-1 pass 10). 3 new function entries added. Key insights: (1) Template execution (templateFn(context)) is distinct from compile() — it throws Handlebars.Exception when a partial referenced in the template is not registered at execution time; (2) strict: true compile option causes template execution to throw on any undefined variable access — common security hardening pattern without proper error handling; (3) prototype access denial (introduced 4.6.0) is a SILENT failure — not an exception but renders blank values, hard to detect; (4) precompile() shares error profile with compile() but is used in build pipelines where developers incorrectly assume the build tool catches exceptions; (5) template() throws on version mismatch between precompile-time and runtime handlebars versions — critical for microservice deployments.
